skill.name: error-handling-patterns
skill.description: Guide error handling design across Python, TypeScript, Rust, and Go using exceptions, Result types, retries, circuit breakers, and graceful degradation.
skill.summary: Design resilient application error handling across common programming languages.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many high-risk patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples and prose, not executable skill logic. The Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance alerts are false positives; the only retained risk factor is documentation-only network request examples.

low_findings.title: Documentation-Only Network Request Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes sample Python and TypeScript network calls used to teach retry and promise error handling. These examples are not executable skill code, but generated implementations should still validate URLs, set timeouts, and avoid sending secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls are direct requests.get and fetch examples inside fenced documentation blocks. I found no evidence that the skill itself performs network access or exfiltrates data.

low_findings.title: Static Execution, Crypto, and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command alerts match Markdown code fences, not Ruby shell execution. The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance alerts match normal prose, filenames, or sample variables about error handling, not crypto APIs or host inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are either Markdown fence delimiters or explanatory examples in a single documentation file. I found no scripts, hidden instructions, prompt injection text, or executable command invocation in the scanned file.

content.value_statement: Unhandled failures make applications brittle and hard to debug. This skill guides structured exceptions, Result types, retries, fallbacks, and recovery patterns across common languages.
actual_capabilities: Explains exception hierarchies for Python and TypeScript applications.
Shows Result and Option patterns for explicit error handling.
Demonstrates retry logic with exponential backoff and timeouts.
Covers circuit breaker, fallback, and graceful degradation patterns.
Compares error handling styles across Python, TypeScript, Rust, and Go.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a runnable analyzer.
It does not include complete production libraries or framework integrations.
Examples need adaptation for each project security model and logging policy.
It does not verify live service behavior or production incident data.

use_cases.title: Design API Failure Behavior
use_cases.description: Define typed errors, status codes, and recovery paths for a new service endpoint.

use_cases.title: Improve Production Reliability
use_cases.description: Add retries, fallbacks, and circuit breakers around unstable dependencies.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Practices
use_cases.description: Create shared conventions for error messages, wrapping, logging, and propagation.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an Error Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between exceptions, Result types, and error codes for this feature. Explain the tradeoffs and recommend one approach.

prompt_templates.title: Review Existing Error Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this error handling flow for swallowed errors, unclear messages, missing cleanup, and poor propagation. Suggest concrete improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Design Resilience Around a Dependency
prompt_templates.prompt: Design retry, timeout, fallback, and circuit breaker behavior for a service that depends on an unreliable external API.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Cross-Language Error Standard
prompt_templates.prompt: Create team guidelines for error types, wrapping, logging, user messages, and observability across Python, TypeScript, Go, and Rust services.

output_examples.input: A payment service sometimes times out and users see generic failures.
output: Recommended pattern: timeout, bounded retry, circuit breaker, and user-safe error message.
Developer action: preserve payment provider context in logs without exposing sensitive data.
User action: show a retryable payment message and avoid duplicate charges.

output_examples.input: A parser returns null when input is invalid.
output: Recommended pattern: explicit Result value for expected validation failures.
Developer action: return structured error details with field names and remediation hints.
Risk reduced: callers must handle invalid input instead of missing silent failures.

output_examples.input: A team catches broad exceptions across many services.
output: Recommended pattern: catch specific errors where recovery is possible.
Developer action: rethrow unexpected failures after adding safe context.
Policy action: reserve broad catches for top-level boundaries and cleanup paths.

best_practices: Handle expected failures explicitly and preserve context for unexpected failures.
Set retry limits, timeouts, and fallback rules before calling external services.
Write user-facing messages separately from developer logs and diagnostic metadata.
anti_patterns: Do not swallow errors without logging, returning context, or taking recovery action.
Do not expose stack traces, tokens, payment details, or internal identifiers to users.
Do not retry every failure without checking idempotency, rate limits, and backoff.

faq.question: Does this skill run code or inspect my system?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill with examples and design guidance.

faq.question: Which languages does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Python, TypeScript, Rust, and Go patterns, plus universal reliability patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with user-facing error messages?
faq.answer: Yes. It encourages clear messages that separate user guidance from internal diagnostics.

faq.question: Does it replace framework-specific documentation?
faq.answer: No. Use it for design direction, then adapt examples to your framework and runtime.

faq.question: Can it help with distributed systems?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes retry, circuit breaker, fallback, and graceful degradation guidance.

faq.question: Is it safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: Yes. Review found documentation examples only, with a low risk note for network call examples.
